item.title: Java 17 Sealed Classes: Close Your Inheritance Graph with permits
item.dc:creator: mario.casari@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
About 7 minutes · Runnable sample: x-posts-examples/java-17-sealed-classes

In Java, almost any class can extend another — unless you mark the parent final. That openness is useful, but it has a downside: you never have a complete list of subtypes. Someone adds class Surprise extends Payment in another module, your switch misses it, and you only find out in production.

Sealed classes (Java 17, JEP 409) fix part of that. You declare a fixed set of allowed subtypes with sealed and permits, and the compiler enforces it. You get a closed hierarchy — not “anything goes,” but “only these types.” Pair that with pattern matching in later Java versions and you can model fixed sets of variants without extra libraries.

Below: how permits works, what final, sealed, and non-sealed mean, a nested example you can run, and the compile errors worth knowing.

The idea in one minute

Imagine a small geometry API. Under Shape you want exactly three direct families: circles, rectangles (with a couple of known subtypes), and a “fancy rectangle” branch that other teams may extend. Without sealing, you write that in Javadoc and hope nobody adds a fourth top-level shape. With sealing, the language enforces it:

public abstract sealed class Shape
    permits Circle, Rectangle, FancyRectangle {

    public abstract double area();
    public abstract String label();
}

Only Circle, Rectangle, and FancyRectangle may extend Shape. Try class Triangle extends Shape and javac rejects it — even in another package, even if you never instantiate it.

That is the whole point: the allowed subclasses are part of the API, checked at compile time, not a team rule buried in wiki pages.

What does permits do?

The permits clause lists every direct subclass. It does not cascade: if Rectangle is on Shape’s list, subtypes of Rectangle are controlled by Rectangle’s own permits (when Rectangle is sealed too).

If all permitted subclasses sit in the same source file as the sealed type, you can omit permits — the compiler infers them. In real codebases I prefer writing permits explicitly; it survives refactors and reads clearly in review.

Sealing works on classes and interfaces. Sealed interfaces are handy for events, commands, or result types where you want a small, fixed set of variants — think “success or failure,” not “and seventeen other things someone might add later.”

Three modifiers — pick one per permitted class

Every name in a permits list must use exactly one of these modifiers. Easy to forget; the compiler will remind you:

ModifierWhat it meansWhen to use it
finalNo subclasses below this type.Leaf types — e.g. Circle, Square.
sealedAnother closed level; needs its own permits.Grouping — e.g. Rectangle permits Square and Oblong.
non-sealedSealing stops here; normal open inheritance below.Plugin or extension point — e.g. FancyRectangle.

List a class in permits but leave it without one of those three? Compilation fails. That stops “permitted but still infinitely extendable” types from slipping through.

A nested example: Shape

This matches the sample project and diagram in x-posts-examples/java-17-sealed-classes:

Shape (abstract sealed)
├── Circle (final)
├── Rectangle (sealed, permits Square, Oblong)
│   ├── Square (final)
│   └── Oblong (final)
└── FancyRectangle (non-sealed)
    └── RoundedRectangle (normal class — not on Shape.permits)

Circle is a leaf — final class Circle extends Shape, done.

Rectangle seals its own branch:

public sealed class Rectangle extends Shape permits Square, Oblong {
    // width, height, area() ...
}

Only Square and Oblong may extend Rectangle. Both are final, so nothing hangs off them.

FancyRectangle is the escape hatch:

public non-sealed class FancyRectangle extends Shape {
    // ...
}

Because it is non-sealed, anyone can subclass it. RoundedRectangle extends FancyRectangle but is not on Shape’s permits list — and that is fine. The top level stays closed (three direct children of Shape); only the branch under FancyRectangle is open again.

Use non-sealed when you want a stable set of top-level variants but still need an extension point somewhere below — a bit like how Exception stays open while many domain types stay closed.

What the compiler actually checks

  • Membership: every direct subclass is on permits (or inferred in the same file).
  • Modifier: each permitted class is final, sealed, or non-sealed.
  • Nested sealing: a sealed class with children must declare permits (or same-file inference).
  • No extras: a type not on the list cannot extend the sealed type.

You see these as red squiggles in the IDE, not as “works on my machine” failures after a new dependency lands on the classpath.

Why bother in real projects?

Domain models. Payments, order statuses, notification types — often a value is exactly one of a few cases. Sealed classes say that out loud in Java, without a third-party sum-type library.

Safer control flow. When the compiler knows all subtypes, it can check that your switch covers every case (Java 21+ pattern matching). On Java 17 you still get a documented, enforced type set — and cleaner instanceof chains. Fewer default branches that silently ignore a new variant.

Stable APIs. You can add a new permitted subtype in a release and make that an explicit API change. With open inheritance, every non-final public class is implicitly “extend me forever.”

Easier reasoning. Closed hierarchies simplify security reviews and deserialization rules: “a Credential is always one of these classes, period.”

Mistakes that fail at compile time

Subclass not on the list.

class Triangle extends Shape { } // error: cannot extend sealed Shape

On the list but missing a modifier.

// abstract sealed class Shape permits Bad { }
class Bad extends Shape { } // error: must be final, sealed, or non-sealed

Sealed middle layer without permits. If Rectangle has subclasses but no permits (and they are not in the same file), it will not compile.

Treating permits as transitive. Putting Rectangle on Shape’s list does not permit Square. Rectangle must list its own children.

Try it yourself

The codingstrain repo has a small Maven module:

cd x-posts-examples/java-17-sealed-classes
mvn -q compile exec:java

It builds a few shapes, prints area and a short label, and shows how instanceof reads when you know the full hierarchy. Sources are under com.codingstrain.sealed.

For a picture of the tree, open x-posts-examples/diagrams/java-17-sealed-classes.drawio in diagrams.net and export a PNG for a featured image. The diagram marks final, sealed, and non-sealed nodes and sums up the compiler rules in one view.

How sealing compares to other options

Enums — great when each variant is a singleton and behavior fits in constants. Sealed classes — when variants carry different fields (Circle has a radius, Oblong has width and height).

Final base class — blocks all extension. Sealed — curated extension: only the types you list.

Javadoc + discipline — what most teams did before Java 17. Sealing lets the compiler join the conversation.

Practical tips

  • Start with the smallest set that fits the domain; add variants on purpose, not “just in case.”
  • final for leaves, sealed for groups, non-sealed only where you really need an open branch.
  • Write permits explicitly when types live in different files.
  • On Java 21+, use exhaustive switch; on 17, keep instanceof handling disciplined.
  • Adding a permitted subtype is usually source-compatible for callers but may break exhaustive switches — treat it like an API change.

Wrapping up

Sealed classes turn “these are all the subtypes” from a comment into something javac enforces. permits says who may join; final, sealed, and non-sealed say what each member may do next. Nest sealing when your domain has structure; use non-sealed when one branch still needs to stay open.

If you model payments, events, or API results as class trees, sealing the root is one of the most useful Java 17 upgrades — no framework change required. Clone the sample, break the rules on purpose and read the errors, and keep the diagram handy — you will internalize the three modifiers faster than memorizing a table.

Further reading: JEP 409 (Sealed Classes), pattern matching for switch in the Java 21 docs, and the java-17-sealed-classes module in codingstrain for code you can run locally.
item.excerpt:encoded: With sealed classes you list exactly who may extend a type. The compiler checks the list — and every permitted subclass must be final, sealed, or non-sealed.
